data.title: Why I Just Became Even More Bullish On The Canton Network
data.description:
Introduction

Blockchains like Ethereum and Solona, among others, allow for something called staking. Owners of ETH or SOL can “stake” their tokens, effectively locking up their tokens, as a means of creating stability for the network. In return, the owners of staked coins receive a yield in the form of the native token. To unstake tokens, the owners may need to wait a few days to over a week. From a tokenomics perspective, the yield incentivizes investors to buy the tokens as a productive asset and the locking mechanism reduces the supply available to be sold on the open market. The idea is that it creates buyer demand and reduces selling.

The ability to stake tokens is glaringly absent from the Canton Network and frankly, I love that it is. Instead of paying out Canton Coin to those that simply buy and stake the coin, all Canton Coin rewards go to those that add value to the network either in the form of building applications or validating transactions. This incentivizes long term value creation on the network rather than speculating on the token. I believe this is hands down a better long-term incentive structure for how rewards should be paid out but this does not address the other benefit to staking which is that it slows the ability to sell. However, the Canton Networks has now taken steps to address this gap with CIP-0105. This approved proposal created a rewards structure that incentivizes the Super Validators on the network to lockup a significant percentage of their past and future Super Validator rewards. While this change did not result in an immediate increase in the price of the coin, I do believe it makes the coin more valuable in the long run.

(Note: This article is largely focused on the CIP-0105 update with little background on the overall network. If you want to gain a deeper understanding of the Canton Network, including some of the risks associated with the Canton Coin, I recommend checking out my previous Seeking Alpha article on the Canton Network which provides a deeper dive into how the network operates)

CIP-0105: Super Validator Locking & Long-Term Commitment Framework

The key parts of the proposal are fairly straight forward. Super Validators must now lock up a percentage of their past and future Super Validator rewards in order to continue to receive rewards going forward. Participation is voluntary, but if the Super Validator elects not to participate, they will no longer receive rewards. The proposal creates three tiers of reward levels laying out what the Super Validators must lock in order to receive 40%, 60% or 100% of their potential Canton Coin rewards for validating transactions. Here’s the breakdown:

As you can see, over the long term this creates an incentive structure where between 35% and 55% of all Super Validator rewards may be locked. If a Super Validator determines that they want to unlock a certain amount of Canton Coin, they can initiate the unlock request at any time but only 1/365.2 of the requested amount will become liquid per day, meaning it will take one year to unlock the full request. This structure aligns Super Validators with the long-term success of the network and reduces the incentive for opportunistic or panic based selling.

Impact on The Available Supply of Canton Coin

The chart above shows the minting curve and reward allocation for Canton Coin. As you can see, by year ten (we are currently in year two) 100 billion coins will have been minted in total and 35 billion of those will go to Super Validators. Based on CIP-0105, we can estimate that somewhere between 35-55% of those coins may be locked. For simplicity’s sake, let’s take the average between the two and say that 45% of the coins will be locked meaning roughly 16 billion Canton Coin will be locked by year ten.

To assess the significance of this, we need to develop an understanding of how many Canton Coins will actually be in existence at year ten. The network burns Canton Coin as part of each transaction on the network. To date, roughly 2.56 billion Canton Coins have been burned which is close to 6% of the total minted supply. If the 6% rate remains constant, by year ten there will be roughly 94 billion Canton Coin in existence. However, the network is currently well outpacing that rate. It is currently burning between 8 and 18 million coins per day while it mints a little over 27 million new coins per day. This means it is currently burning the equivalent of 30-65% of new supply. Over the next eight years, there are roughly 60 billion coins left to be minted before the network stabilizes at minting 2.5 billion coins per year. If the network continues to burn the equivalent of somewhere close to 50% of the new coin supply, this means the network will only add around 30 billion coins over the next 8 years bringing the total supply closer to 70 billion.

I don’t know which of these scenarios will play out so let’s take the average between the two and estimate that eight years from now there will be roughly 83 million Canton Coins in circulation. If 16 billion of those coins are locked, it effectively removes 20% of the supply from circulation. However, if the burn rate ends up remaining closer to 65% of new supply and Super Validators generally elect to lock 55% of their rewards, we could see upwards of 32% of the total supply locked. For comparative context, roughly 30% of ETH are currently staked.

Why This Matters

Obviously, from a tokenomics perspective this decreases the available supply of Canton Coin that could be sold at any given time, and it disincentivizes Supver Validators from panic selling their unlocked supply. But it’s more than that. There are over 40 Super Validators on the Canton Network and CIP-0105 effectively turns each one of them into long term holders of the Canton Coin. The Super Validators are not all just random crypto companies you’ve never heard of. The list of Super Validators includes Nasdaq, Broadridge, Chainlink, Tradeweb, Circle Internet Group, and the Depository Trust and Clearing Corporation.

When it comes to the DTCC alone, we are talking about the highest processor of financial value in the world as they provide custody for over 100 trillion dollars worth of assets. What is key to understand here is that the DTCC is user-owned and directed, meaning their decision to utilize Canton was driven by some form of consensus within the financial community. The Board of Directors at the DTCC has representation from NYSE, JP Morgan, UBS, Citibank, Morgan Stanley, BNY, Goldman Sachs, and Bank of America. This to me looks like bottom-up consensus followed by top-down implementation. If adoption of the Canton Network continues, the network effects will really be incredible to see.

Additionally, I think this increases the chances that Canton Coin will one day be accepted as collateral and that holders of the coin will be able to earn a yield by lending it out. This allows companies holding Canton Coin to maximize the use of their balance sheet. Furthermore (caution: I’d imagine this idea will really drive some Bitcoin maxis up a wall), I think this increases the chances that Canton Coin achieves store of value status. To be clear, we have miles to go before that is achieved and that statement is highly speculative. There is nothing in the Canton White Papers indicating that the aim is to achieve store of value status. The focus is utility. However, the Ethereum Foundation is not shy about the fact that they want to position ETH as a store of value. One of the key features that allows for ETH to potentially become a store of value is staking. With CIP-0105, the Canton Network just took a step in that direction.

Conclusion

The risks with the Canton Network are still numerous. In my opinion, execution risk and the competitive landscape are the top among them. Competition is fierce. There are dozens of other L1 chains looking to gain adoption as a financial settlement layer. While the trend of blockchain adoption is obvious, the winning horse will only appear obvious in hindsight. When it comes to execution, Canton has gone through years of private testing, but it still needs to prove itself at scale.

Despite the risks, there is a lot to like about the network. It is generating fees that are orders of magnitude higher than chains like Ethereum and Solana, yet trades at a network value that is a fraction of where Ethereum and Solana trade. According to data on RWA.xyz, roughly 90% of the total value of tokenized assets (not including stable coins) resides on the Canton Network. Daily transactions on the network have grown from roughly 50 thousand one year ago to over one million. If the Canton Network does earn a place as a key settlement layer for the financial system, it will have been very obvious in hindsight because all of the signs are there now. The CIP-0105 update is just one more feather in the Canton Network's cap to make it even more competitive in relation to other blockchains.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: AGNC Investment: Stable Spreads, Mispriced Volatility Create Income Opportunity
data.description:
AGNC Investment (AGNC) has transitioned into a policy-supported carry vehicle over the past couple of years. The current setup in terms of spreads, funding costs and hedging supports the low teens yields. The recent sell-off since January presents AGNC at ~20% lower prices without a deterioration of its core earnings fundamentals. We are seeing interest rate movements pressuring mark to market book values. Since there is no evidence of spread stability deteriorating (in fact it is in a favorable regime), the volatility in prices could actually offer great entry points for income investors.

Spread Outlook in Carry Regime

The core business model at AGNC is earning from the spread between its investments in long term mortgage backed securities and short term borrowing (repo funding). Since leveraged spreads are the core earnings driver, AGNC sets up hedges against interest rate shocks.

Given this model, AGNC was under structural stress in 2022-23 with rapid increase in interest rates pushing funding costs higher. Volatility in the rate environment made mortgage backed securities difficult to price too. This was bad news for both the carry trade as spreads were unstable and book values were under mark to market losses. This period of share price erosion and lower yields can be seen in the charts below.

Since 2024 we have moved into a regime where spreads, funding, and volatility has stabilized simultaneously. As spreads stabilized, earnings became more predictable. And as volatility declined, book value started holding up. The 2025 spread trends show this stability in spreads despite intra-year fluctuations. To put those fluctuations in perspective, take a look at the spread instability in 2022 and 2023.

Net interest spreads expanded initially to above 3% but then became inconsistent, due to a rapid rise in funding costs and a potential lag in hedge adjustments. During this regime in 2022-23, the cost of funds moved sharply higher from near zero levels to above 1%, and were more importantly under transition - this meant that spreads were not operating within a predictable range, but were instead reacting to macro shocks, making earnings inherently unstable.

In contrast, the data from 2024 (and especially in 2025) shows a very different pattern. Asset yields moved higher and then stabilized in a narrow band around ~4.5% to ~4.9%, while the cost of funds also rose but within a more stable band (eventually stabilizing in the ~2.7% to ~3.1% range). As a result, spreads settled into a tighter band of roughly ~1.8% to ~2.1% through 2025, as seen in the second chart. While there are fluctuations within the year, they look contained.

Having established the relatively stable regime we are in now. Let us dissect the 2025 numbers further to understand the spread outlook. The net interest spread was reported as 1.92% for the year - starting at 2.12% in Q1, compressing to 1.78% in Q3 and stabilizing at 1.81% in Q4. This is deterioration, although mild, and stability seen in Q4 will need to be watched going ahead.

A deeper dive into the breakdown shows nothing structurally alarming at play though (in what is a mild spread compression at best). The asset side of the balance sheet remained remarkably stable through the year, with average asset yields holding in a narrow range of ~4.87% to ~4.95% - the earning power of the mortgage portfolio has not weakened. The compression in spreads is therefore a direct consequence of net funding costs.

However, funding costs have not gone up through the year. Most funding cost elements have actually come down significantly from ~4.5% toward ~4% by Q4. The key driver in the net cost jump is the decline in hedge income. Interest rate swap income, which acts as a buffer against high funding costs, decreased from 1.69% in Q1 to 1.01% in Q4, temporarily pushing the effective cost of funds higher. This is not a model drag, but a drop in hedge income that may continue as markets normalize.

The recent ~20% decline in AGNC’s stock price (from the January 2026 highs) is likely driven by a rise in long term interest rates, increasing discount rates on mortgage assets and leading to mark to market book value pressure in AGNC's leveraged portfolio. Markets may be anticipating some risks around a sustained increase in funding costs or a material rise in interest rate volatility - especially the possibility that interest rates remain higher for longer.

This is an offshoot of the geopolitical tensions and oil prices in particular, keeping inflation higher than expected ahead. That could indeed limit further easing in funding costs and potentially put pressure on spreads. However, the rates have not broken out of the established range since January 2026. It is not likely to come down as sharply as was being potentially assumed earlier - so further easing in funding costs could be under the scanner. But there is no funding stress evident or expected. We are not in a 2022-23 regime that needs a repricing of the underlying spread economics. We could at worst be looking at elevated rates for longer and spreads not improving significantly ahead under the dual impact of hedge benefits diminishing and funding costs remaining elevated - all within a more stable band we have entered into since 2024.

Why It's a Good Time To Accumulate

The current net earnings spread can be expected to stay within the 1.5%-2% band unless interest rates shock upward from here (something like 2022-23 - the odds of which are very low despite macro and geopolitical uncertainties). The lower end of the spectrum when combined with a ~7x leverage AGNC is operating with today, still accounts for a 10-11% RoE ahead, without accounting for dollar roll and hedging contributions (slightly lower now). That means a slightly lower RoE than the current levels of ~15.5%, but still within the band of the improved era we have entered since 2024. Also the ~11% base returns calculated without hedging and dollar roll impacts, roughly translates to ~$1.07 earnings per share, which means a yield of around 11% is looking well covered even in stress scenarios.

The tangible book value stress scenario can be pegged to the lows seen in 2025, say around 15% from current levels. Even if such a scenario arises, the price to book value has already corrected from well above 1.2x to ~1.03x now. That valuation is toward the lower end of the range since 2024. So, valuation compression already seen should be able to buffer any further book value erosion, of up to 15% comfortably.

Summarizing the thesis for AGNC, markets look worried about a higher for longer rate scenario if macro tensions persist and inflation is not brought under control. What AGNC's income thesis requires is stability though, not further improvements in funding costs or spread expansion. Even at ~1.5% spread levels, the model still generates double-digit returns, unlike earlier periods where higher spreads failed due to instability. That makes AGNC a good income addition to your portfolio. Long term investors can start accumulation and keep adding on dips absent material volatility or funding shock events.

data.title: Amkor Technology: Advanced Packaging Turns From A Future Bet To Reality
data.description:
Setting The Stage

Advanced packaging is gaining traction at a CAGR of 8.39%, driven by the demand for high-performance semiconductors. This demand is majorly from electric vehicles (EVs), advanced driver assistance systems (ADAS), and other high-performance products. I am looking at this market tailwind as an anchor on which advanced packaging relies on. This is why I believe Amkor Technology, Inc (AMKR) is continuously positioning itself in this market to seize the opportunity. As we speak, AMKR is the most relied on company in advanced packaging by the world’s leading semiconductor companies in completing their semiconductor designs. From the recently released FY2025 report, Amkor recorded 82.8% of the revenues from advanced products.

AMKR is already in a profitable advanced packaging business at a net income of about $374 million in FY2025, up from $354 million in FY2024. Even more interesting, AMKR is in partnership with NVIDIA, a world-leading chip designer, and will be providing scale advanced packaging at its Arizona facility, scheduled to commence production by 2028. This is a testament that the company is operating on an existing reality heading to other possibilities I will explain later such as Arizona’s new fab.

With this whole semiconductor ecosystem in favour of Amkor, I believe this is why AMKR’s price return is currently at about 136%%, beating the S&P 500, which is at approximately 15% over the last one year.

The Reality In Advanced Packaging

Before I get into AMKR specifics, I will first look at the market tailwinds around advanced packaging. To begin with, let’s look at it this way. Think of an existing business in a rapidly growing market that has hit the road by recording positive YoY growth. But this company has not settled at that point. Instead, it continues to invest more capital to provide advanced products that match the market needs to increase its market access. Realistically, this would be one of pick business to invest in. Isn’t it? I think AMKR is a perfect example of this scenario. Let me put this into context.

My reason is embedded in the current 82.2% of revenues from advanced packaging. This is a signal of market access and the company’s commitment to scale its packaging in the Arizona facility, which is under construction. I will expand this later under financials. But for the sake of my perspective, I am relating Amkor’s revenue growth to the advanced packaging growth rate of a CAGR of 8.39% between 2026 and 2035. The market volume of advanced packaging is estimated to reach $42.96 billion by the end of 2026 and might reach $88.63 billion by 2035.

You may be wondering why I am fixated with the market tailwind, but here is the reason. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM) is one of the Amkor’s trusted partners. TSM is estimated to raise its monthly wafer production in 2026 by 50%. Initially, the TSM had estimated to produce around 26,000 wafers per month in 2026, but has since revised this projection upwards to 127,000 wafers per month by the end of 2026. NVIDIA is one of the companies ordering more than half of TSMC products to a tune of 800,000 to 850,000 wafers yearly, and next year (2027), it might increase its orders as the demand for AI HPCs keeps increasing rapidly. This places Amkor at a winning position as a trusted partner of NVIDIA in advanced packaging.

Now, when you look at the micro perspective, Amkor conducts most of its advanced packaging in Asia, where it has been relatively cheaper to provide these services to TSMC. As we speak, TSMC has started mass production of advanced chips inside US soil. As a result, Amkor and TSMC have signed a memorandum to transfer some advanced packaging operations from Phoenix to Amkor, revealing a long-term trusted partnership. Moving to the execution of the Arizona facility, the facility is expected to skyrocket capital expenditure from $905 million to $2.5-$3.0 billion. My question is whether the company is capable of executing this investment on time. In my view, I believe Amkor is capable because through its increasing free cash flows, driven by rapidly growing advanced packaging sales, and subsidies of $407 million from the federal tax under the CHIPS Act, this may sustain phase 1 of the Arizona facility. The facility is estimated to cost around $7 billion, and the capital expenditure guidance of $3 billion in FY2026 is almost half of this cost. It then indicates that the company is committed to executing this investment on time, which is a reason to be optimistic.

A Look At The Numbers

When it comes to numbers, AMKR is making impressive revenues annually in the upwards of $6 billion YoY. In FY2025, the revenues reached $6.71 billion, representing 6% YoY growth. Despite this increase, Amkor’s revenue growth YoY had a temporary weakness between 2022 and 2023 from 15.53% in 2022 to -8.30% in 2023. However, revenues started to improve in 2024, with the company registering a 6.18% YoY revenue growth in 2025. From my deep dive, there is a myriad of issues that resulted in this decrease, among them being tariffs.

This interrupted a free flow of electronic supplies, especially between the US and Asian countries, where Amkor is conducting most of its advanced packaging. Today, the tariffs have eased following U.S negotiations, and I think this is why the company 6% YoY growth was achieved. The revenue in FY2025 beat analysts’ estimates by 0.73%, signaling that the company has embarked on its upside revenue growth YoY. If all goes well, as previously discussed, Amkor may record an estimated revenue of $7.46 billion in FY2026, representing around 11.22%, which is a double-digit figure.

As previously said on the factors that contributed to the plummeting revenues, the same impacts resulted in declining net income growth in both 2023 and 2024. However, with eased tariffs, the net income has regained a positive trend, now trailing at $373.9 million, representing 5.62% YoY growth. But here, the most interesting observation is that earnings remained resilient because in the two depressing years (2023 and 2024), the earnings still beat analysts’ estimates.

Even more interestingly, the recovering FY2025 as explained above, saw an impressive EPS of $1.50 up from $1.43, representing an earnings surprise of 19.18%. It reveals that Amkor has a strong execution capability and that it has embarked on a profitability trajectory. To put more emphasis here, by the end of 2026, these earnings are likely to hit $1.74 according to consensus estimates, representing 16.24%, which suggests that the company might record improved net income.

Valuation

I am valuing Amkor using its P/E multiple compared to its peers, Applied Materials, Inc (AMAT) and KLA Corporation (KLAC) due to its earnings strength as discussed above. Amkor has the lowest P/E value of 32.09x while AMAT is at 36.59x and KLAC at 43.98x, suggesting that Amkor is undervalued among its peers. But let’s see whether this stands a chance using forward earnings as a clue to this undervaluation.

This undervaluation isn’t justified because Amkor has the highest estimated forward earnings growth of about 36.13% in FY2027 compared to KLAC’s growth of 30.36% and AMAT’s 25.26% . This undervaluation in my view presents a decent entry point for potential investors.

What Could Go Wrong: Massive Capital Expenditure Could Plunge Near-Term Profitability

Amkor’s Arizona is a revenue-generating facility, but at the moment, the projected expenditure could cause financial strain to the company. The company projects a CapEx increase of between $2.5-$3.0 billion in FY2026, which is an increase by almost 3x from FY2025 CapEx, which was around $905 million. Honestly, even though the federal subsidies and tax credits are offered in any case, they lag; this could result in minimal offsets in FY2026’s guidance. I think this is why the company is somewhat conservative on gross margin, because the Q4’2025 was at 16.7%, but now the company projects gross margin of 12.5%-13.5% in Q1’2026. However, this is not a significant risk as it is a fundamental risk worth taking as the company scales its revenue base.

Bottom line

I am reiterating my bullish stance amid Amkor market positioning through partnerships and heavy investment to seize the market opportunity. The semiconductor industry is growing and Amkor has substantial market access, which is why I believe the stock is a buy.

data.title: HYMB: Solid High-Yield Muni Bond ETF, Above-Average Tax-Advantaged Income
data.description:
The SPDR Nuveen Bloomberg High Yield Municipal Bond ETF (NYSEARCA:HYMB) offers investors a tax-advantaged 4.5% dividend yield. HYMB is a solid investment opportunity, a buy, and a particularly interesting choice for investors in taxable accounts facing higher marginal tax rates.

HYMB - Overview and Analysis

Index and Portfolio

HYMB is a high-yield municipal bond index ETF, tracking the Bloomberg Municipal Yield Index (go to 'About this Benchmark' for details). Said index includes relevant dollar-denominated, tax-exempt, muni bonds, subject to a basic set of inclusion criteria. Unlike most high-yield bond indexes, the fund includes sizable investments in quality, investment-grade securities with these accounting for at least 1/3rd of its portfolio.

HYMB offers investors diversified exposure to muni bonds, with investments in almost 2,000 of these, and with exposure to dozens of states, several industries.

HYMB provides diversified exposure to muni bonds. It does not provide diversified exposure to bonds as a whole, as it focuses on a specific bond sub-asset class.

In general, I think that diversification is desirable, and prefer broader bond ETFs like the iShares Flexible Income Active ETF (BINC). In this particular case, I see the merits of focusing on tax-advantaged investments, and hence ETFs like HYMB, although the benefits here are strongly dependent on the specific circumstances of each individual investor.

Risk and Volatility

HYMB's underlying index directly sets portfolio weights by credit ratings. Specifically, index weights are as follows:

  • 70% non-investment grade / unrated securities, so BB+ or lower
  • 20% securities rated BBB
  • 10% securities rated A

Credit quality for the fund is as follows:

HYMB's portfolio weights seem quite close to those of its index, assuming that the vast majority of its unrated bonds are non-investment grade, as is generally the case. HYMB does lean a bit more quality than its index, with around 33% of its portfolio comprising investment-grade securities, even assuming zero quality unrated bonds.

Circling back, HYMB's credit quality seems somewhere between that of high-yield bonds and investment-grade bonds, leaning the former. During downturns and recessions, I would expect a moderate amount of losses, some outperformance relative to high-yield bonds, some underperformance relative to higher-quality securities.

HYMB saw a 15% drawdown during the pandemic, performing between investment-grade and high-yield bond index ETFs, closer to the latter. Results were consistent with the fund's portfolio quality.

Looking at rate risk, the fund has a duration of 7.9 years, an above-average figure, somewhat high on an absolute basis, but far from long-term treasuries and the like. Compare figures for HYMB and index ETFs tracking the major bond sub-asset classes:

Due to the above, expect above-average sensitivity to rates for HYMB, resulting in outperformance when rates decline, underperformance when these decrease. HYMB outperformed during the most recent cutting cycle, as expected.

HYMB has above-average credit risk, on account of its significant high-yield bond investments. At the same time, rate risk is above-average, as evidenced by duration figures. Realized volatility should be above-average too, although that does not seem to be the case, at least not consistently.

As can be seen above, HYMB's realized volatility is close to the bond average right now, with some significant short-term fluctuations on account of the ongoing Iranian military action. I'm seeing several fluctuations in prior years too, although HYMB's realized volatility rarely goes above the bond average.

Overall, HYMB's fundamentals are those of a somewhat risky fund, actual realized volatility about average, perhaps slightly below-average. Results seem a bit better than expected, but nothing out of the ordinary.

Dividend Analysis

HYMB's 4.5% dividend yield is comparable to that of investment-grade corporate bonds, quite a bit lower than that of high-yield corporate bonds. Considering the fund's credit risk, the latter is the more appropriate comparison, but due remember that the fund does have sizable quality holdings, and that realized volatility is about average.

HYMB's dividends are also tax-advantaged, as income from muni bonds is generally exempt from federal income taxes. Specifics vary, the fund should offer particularly attractive after-tax income for investors in taxable accounts and in the higher tax brackets. Investors in tax-advantaged accounts, or in the lower tax brackets, might see higher income from other ETFs, however.

Breakeven taxable equivalent yields, or tax-free yields, might give investors greater clarity on the above. Figures, and an explanation of these, follow:

In the table above, the figure highlighted in yellow tells us that for investors in the 35.8% tax bracket, a tax-free 4.5 dividend yield, equivalent to that of HYMB, generates as much in income as a taxable 7.0% dividend yield (or 4.5% = 7.0% * (100% - 35.8%)). High-yield corporate bond ETFs tend to yield around that, generally slightly lower, with the benchmark iShares Broad USD High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (USHY) yielding 6.8%.

So, investors in taxable accounts facing a 35.8% income tax should see higher after-tax income from HYMB than from most bond ETFs, including those centered on high-yield corporate bonds. For these investors, HYMB seems like an interesting choice. Same is true for investors in higher tax brackets. Investors in lower tax brackets, however, might prefer taxable high-yield bonds, as these seem likely to offer higher after-tax income than HYMB. Same is true for investors in tax-advantaged accounts.

Notwithstanding the above, I would like to emphasize that these issues or considerations are ultimately dependent on the specific circumstances of each individual investor. There are bound to be exceptions, so consider these generalities as a starting point. Although obviously the suitability of an investment is always somewhat dependent on the investor, I think that this issue is of much greater importance for muni bonds in general, HYMB in particular, than most.

Performance Track-Record

HYMB's performance track-record seems adequate. Figures are as follows:

Several things stand out from the above.

Long-term returns are closest to investment-grade corporate bonds, consistent with dividend yields. Returns are lower than those of high-yield corporate bonds, the closest taxable fixed-rate analogue to HYMB.

Performance has been quite poor these past five years, as these coincide with the massive increase in rates that started in 2022. Hikes set the stage for higher returns long-term, due to higher rates across fixed-income markets.

Performance has materially improved these past three years, however, as market rates had more or less stabilized by early 2023.

Returns have been surprisingly poor these past twelve months, as muni bonds seem to have suffered quite a bit more than expected from the market turmoil of early 2025, caused by the imposition of tariffs. I'm not sure that this move is fully rational, but some variation or volatility in fixed-income markets is normal.

Figures above are before-tax, so don't take into consideration HYMB's tax advantages. After-tax returns should be lower for some of the other ETFs, below those of HYMB for lots of investors. As always, tax considerations are ultimately dependent on the specific circumstances of each individual investor.

Overall, HYMB's performance track-record is perfectly adequate. It's investment thesis rests on its potential tax advantages, potentially strong after-tax income. It's performance track-record does not seem strong enough to be part of the fund's investment thesis, or strong enough to merit a buy rating, but it does not detract from the fund's advantages either.

Conclusion

HYMB is a high-yield bond muni bond ETF, with a good tax-advantaged 4.5% dividend yield. Said dividend yield should result in strong, above-average after-tax income for many investors, making the fund a buy. As the fund's advantages and investment thesis are dependent on the specific circumstances of each investor, it is not an investment for everyone, some might prefer other alternatives.

data.title: The 90% Tax Era: What Investors Should Know Today
data.description:
90% tax rates probably aren't coming back

Many in the "Roth only" camp frequently hearken back to an earlier time to observe where tax rates have come down from. Pulling a page out of Ed Slott's excellent book The Retirement Savings Tax Bomb Ticks Louder, we can see on page 28 a tidy historical record of the top marginal rates:

Now the top marginal rate is always the one highlighted to instill fear in the public. The truth is very few enter into these brackets, especially when planning efficiently with various tax timing strategies regardless of your account mix. However, interesting patterns emerge. The rolling 20s that led to the Great Depression had a very friendly tax environment with a top marginal rate of 25% from 1925 to 1931.

This top marginal rate strategy of 25% was led by then Treasury Secretary Andrew Mellon. This was the first time supply-side trickle down-type economics came into the picture. The belief was that less taxes leads to more spending, which ultimately leads to more tax revenue. The wealthy keep more, create more jobs, and it gives the power to private capitalist society to guide the direction of the economy.

It's apparent in hindsight that the lower top marginal rates also led to the top echelon of society not pouring everything into industrial capacity, but much of it went into stocks.

Back to the 90% era effective tax rates

While a top marginal rate over 90% is scary, is it all just fear mongering? Or were the lower brackets draconian as well? Let's take a look:

It turns out the 90+% era wasn't just scary; it was terrifying. Here we can see the lowest rate in 1945 was 23%! In 2026, you don't hit the 22% bracket if married filing jointly until $100,800.

The lowest rates at 10% after a very generous $32,200 standard deduction for 2026 are some of the best deals of all time. This is why tax experts and CPAs like Ed Slott believe that going heavy into Roth accounts now is indeed wise, as you are likely to have higher tax brackets in retirement if your primary nest egg is a pre-tax account and social security. As Ed says in his book, if you have a traditional IRA, 401k, 457, or 403b, rather than a Roth version, your basis is zero.

Every dollar that comes out will be taxed at ordinary income rates and also affect other things like IRMAA, which is determined by modified adjusted gross income [MAGI]. IRMAA is the surcharge on Medicare parts A and B if you go over a certain level of modified adjusted gross income.

Social Security hit

The other item where MAGI and provisional income come into play is Social Security. The formula is: tax-exempt interest + 50% of SS benefits + AGI [excluding SS]. This is what happens when that amount crosses certain thresholds:

Not even tax-exempt municipal bonds can save you here. Once provisional income of more than $44,000 gets hit, 85% of the benefit is taxed at your marginal rate. Added senior standard deductions help here, but there's no telling how long that will last.

The extra senior deduction

Right now through 2028, there's an extra senior standard deduction of $12,000 if married filing jointly. This stacks on top of the $32,200 and the $3,300 additional senior deduction for joint filers. Thus the first $47,500 for a couple 65 or older could be covered.

This gets phased out at $150,000 for joint filers. Again, this is based on MAGI, not taxable income.

A couple hypothetical situation

The Traditional Account Example

Let's say a couple is 65, they only have traditional accounts, and they have a $45,000/year Social Security benefit. They need to pull $100,000 from the traditional account in addition to their benefit.

Provisional income would be: $100,000 [remember, zero basis] +$22,500 [half of SS] = $122,500.

Now this gets into the 85% threshold, so $38,250 of the benefit also becomes taxable income.

AGI now becomes $100,000+$38,250 for a grand total of $138,250.

The total deduction is $47,500, leaving $90,750 of taxable income. If this falls into the 12% bracket, estimated tax due would be around $10,394, effectively going through the 10% and 12% brackets.

The Roth example

Roth is non-existent income regardless of the amount that comes out, it doesn't hit your tax return post 59.5 for contributions, conversions, or gain amounts.

Provisional income in this situation is $0 even though the same $100k comes out. Add this $0 to 1/2 SS equals $22,500 total. This is below $32,000, 0% of SS is taxable in this case.

AGI= $0 in this case.

Tax= $0 at the Federal level.

The Roth saved $10,000 in taxes.

What if rates rise?

If tax rates for the bottom went to, say, 15% and 25% for the first two brackets your money passes through, here's the effect. The hypothetical 15%/25% brackets would end up leaving you with a roughly $20,000 tax bill.

Roth conversion opportunities

So, financial planners and CPAs are emphasizing this moment in history, [the Trump timeline through 2028], to convert as much as possible. With the large standard deduction and low tax brackets, if one could live on $100,000 or so a year [$50,000 or so from traditional accounts and the rest from social security], the remaining $60,000 up to not hitting that $150k phase-out range for the senior bonus or the 22% bracket could be used for Roth conversions.

This would end up looking something like this:

$50,000 IRA income + $38,500 taxable SS = $88,250 MAGI.

$88,250 + $60,000 Roth conversion = $148,300 total MAGI.

Minus the $47,500 standard deduction leaves exactly $100,800 in taxable income. You've just converted $60,000 to your Roth accounts [with no future RMDs] and filled up the 12% bracket. Of course this is cutting it close, you also could use something like QCDs [qualified charitable distributions] at the end of the year to get you back within your ranges. Or just be more conservative on withdrawals.

Moving growth assets in kind

So if you've mapped out your Roth conversion strategy, what should be moved if it's not realistic to move everything before RMDs [required minimum distributions] begin? It's probably best to move your growth assets. The ones that continue to grow and compound will cause the most headaches when RMDs come.

Many brokers now offer in-kind conversions. If so, you can move a set dollar amount of shares of your choosing over to your Roth.

60/40? Move the 60

Chances are if you have an advisor, they've probably put you into a more conservative allocation of 60% equities and 40% fixed income by retirement. Using Vanguard for examples, your make up of funds could look like this :

Asset Class Category Allocation Vanguard Ticker
U.S. Equities Large Growth 16% (VUG)
Large Value 16% (VTV)
Mid Cap 3.25% (VO)
Small Cap 3.25% (VB)
International Developed Markets 14.5% (VEA)
Emerging Markets 4.5% (VWO)
Real Estate U.S. REITs 2.5% (VNQ)
U.S. Bonds Government (Intermediate) 8% (VGIT)
Corporate 16.25% (VTC)
High Yield 4% (VCEB)
Municipal 1% (VTEB)
Intl Bonds Total Intl Bond 4.75% (BNDX)
Cash Money Market 6% (VUSXX)

So in a hypothetical in-kind conversion scenario, everything above the U.S. Bond line item could be targets for conversions. These are the assets that will be most likely to grow and the ones you probably don't want subject to future RMDs.

It's good to look at your accounts holistically rather than individually. Not every account has to be 60/40 equities/bonds. As long as your total exposure meets your targets across all accounts is what matters most. By the end of conversions, one might have a $600,000 all equity Roth account and a $400,000 all bond account. This is still a 60/40 portfolio overall.

Is the U.S. government backed into a corner?

I guess the final questions are, first, is the United States really backed into a corner? Post-World War II Americans were very patriotic, and inflation was under control. Those ultra-high tax rates were stomached to keep the government running and strong. I have a feeling that no such sentiment will exist ever again. Not that rates won't ever rise substantially, but that there will be protests about it and they will get members of Congress un-elected pretty quick.

The more likely scenario is slight increases, continued stimulus and money printing. The inflation will continue to prop up assets and continue to widen the chasm between those with and without assets. Just my opinion, but I could be entirely wrong.

Will Congress be banned from trading stocks?

The idea of not allowing Congress to trade stocks has been floated. If they are outright banned from owning them like certain executive appointees or Federal Reserve members, then the law makers might become more indifferent to rates rising. Right now everyone is in the same boat.

Summary

Ed Slott and other's opinion that Roth conversions sit in a golden window until 2028 is not without merit. As the wealth divide widens, states will probably take the first initiative and the Federal government will follow later if those in power are not those that believe in trickle down economics. Life is long and it is highly probable that we eventually get an administration in favor of raising taxes and subsidizing those that already pay little tax which make up the majority.

data.title: General Mills: A Gradual Shift In Narrative, But Not A Quick Turnaround
data.description:
The past 12-month period has been quite challenging for consumer staples and packaged food companies in particular. Ongoing cost pressures are forcing businesses to undergo yet another round of price increases and this is taking its toll on volumes, margins and investors' confidence as well.

General Mills (GIS) has not been immune to these trends and the stock's performance over the past year reflects that. After the Q3 2026 report, it seems that there won't be any changes to the narrative in the near-future as the management reaffirmed its full fiscal year outlook for a low single-digit sales drop and a material drop in both operating profit and earnings per share.

While this could be interpreted as neither good nor bad news, the GIS stock has fallen by more than 36% over the past year and is one of the worst performers within the already challenged peer group.

As a result, GIS is now trading single digit earnings multiple on a GAAP basis and at a forward P/E multiple of 11 once non-recurring items are accounted for.

On one hand, we now have a stock that is trading at ridiculously low levels, but on the other the latest report has sent a signal to investors that a recovery in the coming quarters is unlikely.

Having said that, long-term oriented investors should pay attention as the recent quarter has also shown some early positive signs which coupled with the record-low valuation could present a good entry point.

Before we proceed to some of the specifics from the quarter, it should be mentioned that I warned investors about some company-specific risks back in October of last year. These were primarily related to capital allocation decisions and certain operational metrics. While trends in the latter might change in the short-term, mistakes within the capital allocation process are much harder to remedy.

Lackluster Performance

On the surface, the third quarter of FY 2026 did not present investors with anything of substance. The full fiscal year guidance was reaffirmed and the quarterly results were roughly in-line with the expectations of a notable drop in profitability.

The stock fell on the day of the report as organic sales growth has fallen to a negative 3% after the previous report has shown that the decline could be soon coming to an end (note the jump in Q2 of that year).

Organic volumes fell by 1% in Q1 of 2026 and then it seemed as if they will be gradually recovering after a 0% impact on volumes in Q2. During the last report, however, GIS returned to negative volume growth and this is now causing unease among investors as the company's organic price/mix is also in negative territory.

We could see the dynamic between volumes and price/mix over the past 5 years on the graph below, which clearly illustrates the performance issues that GIS has had over the course of the past 12 months.

Back in October of last year, I criticized the company's approach within the Pet Foods segment that involved a number of M&A deals and a fragmented brand strategy. While I remain sceptical of that approach, the recent quarter has shown some progress in that area with retail sales accelerating to 2.4%. It is too early to judge whether or not this is a sustainable trend, but it is worth noting.

The drop in quarterly gross margin, however, is quite concerning as it follows on the trend of organic sales growth that came below the levels from the prior quarter.

During the quarter, GIS management expressed some optimism that they are seeing some early signs of these trends changing, but so far there is hardly anything to act upon.

And as we look ahead to fiscal ‘27, with the headwind from base price adjustments behind us and with plans to further advance the remarkability of our brands, we’re confident that we can deliver improved organic sales growth while continuing to generate industry-leading cost efficiency.

Source: GIS Q3 2026 Prepared Remarks

Is It Cheap?

Looking at valuation multiples in isolation, GIS looks very cheap. The stock is now trading at single-digit earnings multiple which is quite rare in today's richly priced equity market.

The GAAP earnings, however, include a $1bn divestment gain, which distorts the earnings multiple.

As we saw on a Non-GAAP forward basis, GIS trades at 11 times earnings, which albeit higher is still very attractive pricing when compared to the rest of the equity market.

The same is confirmed when we look at GIS historical price/sales multiple in comparison to the company's operating margin. For most large and mature businesses, these two variables usually exhibit relatively strong relationship over time. In the case of GIS, the current P/S multiple of 1.1 is at its lowest levels since 2011, while margins have been at similar levels back in 2015.

Overall, on a long-term basis it is quite hard to make the case that GIS will remain priced at roughly 1 times sales and 11 times earnings for a very long period of time. Even after we account for the ongoing difficulties outlined above, a scenario where operating margins continue to deteriorate and settle permanently at much lower levels is very unlikely.

The issue is that in the short-term investors should anticipate more volatility and that does not bode well for investors in consumer staples, who usually look for stability and attractive dividends.

When it comes to the latter, it is now at risk of being reduced in the coming year provided that the current conditions do not improve. The yield of 6.5% confirms that and it is one of the highest within the large cap Packaged Food space.

When comparing the cash payout ratios of the 4 companies listed above it becomes clear why is the market now pricing-in the risk of a cut going forward.

Even though GIS capital expenditure has been reduced in recent quarters and is now below the annual depreciation and amortization expense, the free cash flow is just barely above the annual dividend cash outflow.

Investor Takeaway

The most recent quarter did not offer any meaningful change in the narrative around GIS. The company is expected to continue to face operational headwinds in the near-term, albeit there are some early positive signs that in FY 2027 we could see growth improving. After taking into account the exceptionally low pricing of the GIS stock, we could make the case for GIS being a cautious long-term buy. Nonetheless, due to the risk for the dividend and certain capital allocation mistakes, I am reluctant to upgrade the stock at this point in time.

data.title: Regal Rexnord: Not An Ideal Prospect, Good Enough To Remain Bullish
data.description:
Almost three years ago, in May of 2025, I wrote my last article about Regal Rexnord Corporation (RRX). At the time, I found it to be an interesting prospect, even though revenue, profits, and cash flows, had experienced weakness. The stock was decently attractive from a valuation standpoint. This was true on both an absolute basis and relative to other similar companies. And I believed that, in the long run, this play would ultimately work out. Since then, however, the stock has underperformed the market. It's up only 47.1% while the S&P 500 is up 58%. Considering that I rated it a ‘buy’, I would say that it has fallen short of my expectations.

The good news is that the picture for the business is improving. At first glance, financial performance looks mixed, but when you dig deeper, things are going surprisingly well. Management has also set forth an ambitious plan for the next couple of years, and key parts of the company are expanding nicely. Add on top of this the fact that while the stock might not be the cheapest on an absolute basis, it is cheap compared to other similar firms, and I believe that maintaining it as a ‘buy’ makes all the sense in the world right now.

Taking a new look at Regal Rexnord

Given the amount of time that has passed since my last article about Regal Rexnord, I think it would be appropriate to talk a bit about exactly what the company does. At its core, the company focuses on certain power systems. But to best understand it, it would be wise to look at each of its operating segments in turn. The largest of these at the end of 2025 ended up being its Industrial Powertrain Solutions segment. Through this unit, the company offers highly engineered transmission products such as mounted and unmounted bearings, couplings, mechanical power transmission drives, clutches, brakes, and certain industrial powertrain components.

You also have the Automation & Motion Control segment. This unit produces and services conveyor products, aerospace components, precision motion control solutions, drives and linear actuators, and more. Unlike the Industrial Powertrain Solutions segment that focuses on catering to the general industrial market, the metals and mining industry, and the energy space, not to mention things like commercial HVAC, this segment counts food and beverage companies, aerospace firms, medical centers, and data centers, as its customer base. And then finally, we have the Power Efficiency Solutions segment. This unit produces fractional and other horsepower motors, electronic drives, fans, blowers, and other offerings. A lot of what it does is dedicated to the residential and commercial HVAC

Over the last few years, the financial picture for the company has been mixed. Revenue actually dropped from $6.25 billion in 2023 to $5.93 billion in 2025. At first glance, this seems distressing. But there is some good news here. And this is the fact that this drop in revenue was driven in large part by the company's decision to sell off its industrial motors and generators businesses. That transaction was ultimately completed in April of 2024. For context, revenue for these operations amounted to $521.5 million in 2023. So if we strip this out from the equation, revenue would have actually risen over the last three years from $5.73 billion to $5.93 billion.

Overall profitability for the company has been mixed, but I would say it has been slightly better than worse. For instance, the business went from a net loss of $57.4 million in 2023 to a net profit of $279.5 million last year. Operating cash flow expanded from $715.3 million to $990.8 million. And if we strip out changes in working capital, we would get an increase from $618.9 million to $786.3 million. Finally, EBITDA remained flat at $1.31 billion. That is certainly disappointing to say the least.

The company’s expansion has been the result of strength across two of its three remaining operating segments. The Automation & Motion Control segment, as an example, grew from $1.52 billion to $1.69 billion over the last three years. It appears as though greater demand in the aerospace and discrete automation markets proved to be a benefit to the company during this time. But there were areas of weakness. This included the general industrial and medical end markets, not to mention certain challenges involving the rare earth magnets space for the medical and defense industries. Unfortunately, operating profits actually declined, dropping from $139 million to $133.9 million. But management did not really provide much in the way of detail on that.

The other growth area for the company, and the one that has been particularly impressive, is the Industrial Powertrain Solutions market. Revenue grew from $2.40 billion to $2.59 billion. However, it is worth noting that 2025 was actually worse than 2024 was because of weakness in the general industrial space that more than countered strength in the energy industry. But from 2023 to 2024, revenue jumped 8.1%, though that was only because of acquisition activities. That added $243.2 million during that year, with the acquisition in question being Altra Industrial Motion, a business that operates as a producer of highly engineered products and subsystems in the factory automation and industrial power transmission markets.

This cost shareholders $5.13 billion, which is massive compared to the current market capitalization of Regal Rexnord of $12.48 billion and its enterprise value of $16.76 billion. The good news is that as revenue here expanded, overall profits skyrocketed. In fact, management expects that, by 2027, it will have captured $250 million of cross sell synergies between this purchase and one other. And that's not even factoring in other cost cutting initiatives that could improve bottom line results.

The weak spot for the company from a revenue standpoint ended up being its Power Efficiency Solutions segment. Revenue here dropped from $1.81 billion to $1.65 billion. And as that occurred, operating income dipped from $217.4 million to $209.4 million. The overall drop in revenue was primarily because of organic sales declines, with management attributing that to a weaker residential HVAC market, as well as a reduction in activity involving general commercial and non-US commercial HVAC spaces. This actually doesn't surprise me. As I have detailed in other articles, the housing market is especially weak. And with broader economic conditions weakening, pain in the commercial and non-US commercial HVAC spaces is to be expected.

When you really step back and look at what is going on here, there are reasons to be optimistic. The fact that revenue, adjusted for asset sales, actually increased over the last three years is encouraging. And the general uptrend in cash flow is promising also. Management continues to benefit from a rise in backlog as well. It went from $1.88 billion in 2023 to $2.54 billion last year. The company is also targeting certain growth opportunities. This includes by banking on the data center market, which is responsible for just 3% of overall revenue. This is a rapid growth segment, with cloud computing demand expected to experience a growth in revenue from $496 billion in 2023 to $2 trillion by 2030. So clearly, the company stands to benefit in this regard. But you also have other attractive spaces, like the discrete automation market that accounts for 21% of revenue. Labor shortages and labor inflation, not to mention continued investments in robotics, should serve as catalysts here. Electrification and the rise of eVTOL fleets should spur further demand in the aerospace and defense market that accounts for 7% of the company's revenue. And then when you add on top of this the fact that only 2% of the firm's revenue comes from the medical space, which is primed to benefit from robotic surgery adoption, lab automation, and high precision tool adoption, and there are reasons to be bullish in the long run.

The data center market is a really attractive space that the company is already seeing some great progress in. In 2025, its Automation & Motion Control segment received $1 billion of orders, with its E-Pods custom modular scalable turnkey power management solutions offerings experiencing $735 million of those orders. It also has a $600 million bid pipeline, not to mention a $100 million bid pipeline when it comes to its Power Efficiency Solutions business.

The stock is not particularly cheap. But it is worth noting that management anticipates revenue growth this year and next. They believe, for instance, that by 2027, revenue will hit between $6.4 billion and $7 billion. In the event that management hits this target, we are looking at EBITDA of about $1.675 billion. That would be based in part on margin expansion amounting to 3% of sales. This would likely translate to adjusted operating cash flow of about $1.01 billion.

Using these estimates, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are priced at the moment. The stock is not cheap, but it is far from expensive. And in the same chart, you can see that it is cheaper than shares of other comparable enterprises. But even if we ignore the possibility of its trading multiples expanding, especially as revenue and profit margins improve, there is still some market beating potential that exists here. For instance, if the company achieves its targets for 2027, and if it continues to trade at the same multiples it's trading at today, we would be looking at annualized upside, using the price to adjusted operating cash flow approach, of 15.3%. And if we use the EV to EBITDA approach, annualized upside would be 20%. Even coming in at the low end of this range would put it above what the market would be expected to achieve.

Takeaway

Based on the data provided right now, it is my belief that Regal Rexnord is not an ideal prospect. However, it's good enough to remain bullish about. Management has significantly reinvented the enterprise over the last couple of years, and that reinvention process is not over. Long term, I believe that further upside potential is likely, not only through trading multiple expansion, but through margin expansion. When you add all of this together, I believe that maintaining the company as a ‘buy’ makes all the sense in the world here.
